ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general crab food entity; no specific BBQ/grilled whole crab entry exists. Mapped to the closest broader crab food class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crab broadly; no BBQ-specific crab code exists. Adapted from the cooked crab category as the closest defensible match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 is a substance-level code for crab; no preparation-specific code for BBQ crab exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole extra-large crab weighing approximately 850 g, as typically served at a Singapore seafood restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food Database
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (blue crab, cooked, moist heat) as the closest approved-source equivalent for a whole cooked crab. Sodium is elevated above the USDA baseline (97 mg/100g) to approximately 294 mg/100g to account for the BBQ sauce marinade (soy sauce base), consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on soy-sauce-marinated seafood dishes. Fat is similarly adjusted upward from the USDA baseline (1.8 g/100g) to 7.1 g/100g to account for oil and sauce coating during BBQ preparation. All adjustments are documented here. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA and HPB data and retained where plausible.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: BBQ Crab (XL) contains only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is warranted or available for this food type. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0. glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to signal that GI measurement is not meaningful for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from BBQ/soy sauce marinade (294 mg/100 g; ~2,499 mg per 850 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium per serving (~2,499 mg, approaching the daily 2,000 mg limit in a single meal); request BBQ sauce on the side and avoid additional soy-based dipping condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.8 g/100 g) and negligible sugar (0.9 g/100 g) with high protein (14.1 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: BBQ Crab is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate and glycaemic impact; however, the BBQ sauce contains added sugar and soy sauce, so requesting light sauce application is advisable for strict blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (117 mg/100 g; ~994 mg per serving) and high sodium (294 mg/100 g) from BBQ sauce, partially offset by lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids from crab
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the high dietary cholesterol and sodium per serving are significant concerns, and portion control with reduced sauce application is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 and zinc from crab offset by high sodium and nightshade ingredients in BBQ sauce
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Crab provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and zinc for RA management, but the high sodium from BBQ sauce may worsen tissue swelling; nightshade-sensitive individuals should monitor joint response to chili and other nightshade components in the sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Crab (Extra Large, Whole) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean seafood dish featuring a single live extra-large crab (800–1000 g, served at approximately 850 g) that is marinated and grilled or barbecued over high heat with a savory BBQ sauce typically made from soy sauce, garlic, ginger, chili, and sesame oil. The dish is known for its smoky, umami-rich flavor profile, with the natural sweetness of fresh crab meat complemented by the caramelized, slightly spicy BBQ glaze. The crab shell develops a charred, aromatic crust while the interior meat remains succulent and tender. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 123 kcal, 14.1 g protein, 7.1 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 1.8 g carbohydrate, and 294 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 2,499 mg per whole crab serving) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Cholesterol is elevated at approximately 117 mg/100 g (~994 mg per serving), relevant for cardiovascular risk management. The very low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management with minimal glycaemic impact. Crab is a natural source of omega-3 fatty acids, zinc, and selenium, which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is commonly served at Singapore seafood restaurants and hawker centres, often as a shared centerpiece dish during family meals or celebratory occasions. It is also known in Chinese as 烧烤螃蟹 and in Malay as Ketam BBQ.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a whole BBQ crab?
Is BBQ crab high in sodium?
Is grilled crab good for weight loss?
How much protein in BBQ crab?
Can hypertension patients eat BBQ crab?

confidence_reason: Core macros are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (blue crab, cooked, moist heat) with sodium and fat adjusted upward to account for BBQ sauce marinade. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source; values were cross-checked against USDA and HPB data. No exact BBQ whole crab entry exists in any approved primary source, so this record is an adapted estimate. Confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for BBQ-marinated whole crab is not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180) or the Singapore HPB database at the dish level as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reliably available for this specific BBQ-prepared whole crab dish in approved sources; USDA FDC ID 175180 provides some values for plain cooked crab but the BBQ sauce preparation introduces variability that cannot be reliably quantified without dish-specific laboratory analysis. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100 g based on USDA FDC ID 175180 baseline (~259 mg/100 g for plain cooked blue crab) adjusted for sauce contribution. Trans fat reported at 0.06 g/100 g from input record; this is a trace level consistent with minor contributions from cooking oils used in BBQ preparation. Glycemic index is set to numeric value 0 as a sentinel to satisfy numeric field requirements; gi_category is 'not_applicable' because this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.8 g/100 g) and GI measurement is not meaningful. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not qualify as an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore were used instead.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The sole failure from iteration 2 (q4: glycemic_index must be numeric) is resolved by setting glycemic_index to the numeric sentinel value 0 while retaining gi_category 'not_applicable' to correctly signal that GI measurement is not meaningful for this low-carbohydrate food. Nutrient data adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 with documented adjustments for BBQ sauce. All mandatory Singaporean-dish multilingual names present. All -1 fields documented in notes. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object.
